Question ID: coding-S1-ahc001-atcoder-ad
Title: S1. AHC001 / AtCoder Ad

Problem:
Place many non-overlapping axis-aligned ad rectangles in a 10000 x 10000 square so each advertiser gets a rectangle containing its requested point and with area close to its requested target area.

Grading target:
Continuous score from advertiser satisfaction.
Higher is better.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

Node started: Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-S1-ahc001-atcoder-ad
Title: S1. AHC001 / AtCoder Ad

Problem:
Place many non-overlapping axis-aligned ad rectangles in a 10000 x 10000 square so each advertiser gets a rectangle containing its requested point and with area close to its requested target area.

Grading target:
Continuous score from advertiser satisfaction.
Higher is better.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

Node started: Develop an algorithm to determine the optimal sequence and spatial boundaries for the rectangles. Given the 10000x10000 grid and N points (xi, yi) with target areas Ai, implement a recursive quadtree-style decomposition or a sweep-line partitioning strategy. The goal is to divide the 10000x10000 space into N non-overlapping rectangular cells Ci such that each Ci contains its respective point (xi, yi). Use a priority queue to process points based on their target area Ai (e.g., largest area first) to ensure high-value advertisers are allocated space before the grid becomes too fragmented.

Node started: For each assigned cell Ci (defined by [xmin, xmax] x [ymin, ymax]) and its target area Ai, calculate the dimensions of the final rectangle Ri. The rectangle Ri must satisfy: 1) Ri is a subset of Ci, 2) (xi, yi) is in Ri, and 3) Area(Ri) is approximately Ai. Implement a logic that maximizes Area(Ri) up to Ai by expanding from (xi, yi) towards the boundaries of Ci. Specifically, solve for width w and height h such that w * h = min(Ai, Area(Ci)), while ensuring xi - w/2 >= xmin, xi + w/2 <= xmax, etc. If a perfect aspect ratio is impossible, use a greedy expansion that prioritizes the dimension with the most remaining slack.
